commit.message:
[cDAC] Add typed field extension methods for Data classes (#126713)

## Summary

Add typed field extension methods for the cDAC managed reader and
convert all `IData<T>` classes to use them. This builds on the native
data descriptor type system (#126163) to add managed-side type
validation.

### Changes

**`TargetFieldExtensions.cs`** — New extension methods on `Target`:
- `ReadField<T>` — reads a primitive field with `Debug.Assert` type
validation
- `ReadPointerField` / `ReadPointerFieldOrNull` — reads pointer fields
(required/optional)
- `ReadNUIntField` — reads native unsigned integer fields
- `ReadCodePointerField` — reads code pointer fields
- `ReadDataField<T>` / `ReadDataFieldPointer<T>` — reads
inline/pointer-to Data struct fields
- `ReadFieldOrDefault<T>` — reads optional primitive fields with default
value

When the data descriptor includes type information (debug/checked
builds), these methods assert that the declared field type is compatible
with the C# read type.

**All ~130 `IData<T>` classes converted** to use the new extension
methods, replacing direct `target.Read<T>(address +
(ulong)type.Fields[...].Offset)` calls.

### Bugs found and fixed

The type system caught several pre-existing issues:
1. **DynamicStaticsInfo.GCStatics/NonGCStatics** — descriptor said
`uint32`, native type is `TADDR` (pointer)
2. **EEClass.FieldDescList** — used `Read<ulong>` for a pointer field
(broken on 32-bit)
3. **ExceptionClause.ClassToken** — read `TypeHandle` (nuint) as `uint`
4. **SimpleComCallWrapper.RefCount** — property was `ulong`, native is
`LONGLONG` (signed)
5. **NativeCodeVersionNode.NativeCode** — descriptor was `T_POINTER`,
actually `CodePointer`
6. **MethodTable.EEClassOrCanonMT** — descriptor was `T_NUINT`, managed
reads as pointer

> [!NOTE]
> This content was generated with the assistance of GitHub Copilot.

---------

Co-authored-by: Max Charlamb <maxcharlamb@microsoft.com>

commit.message:
feat(runtime): enable no-timeout WASM select parity (#910)

* feat(wasm): unlock select parity via cooperative scheduler

Implements hew_select_first and hew_reply_wait in the WASM reply-channel
module (reply_channel_wasm.rs) and removes the five hew.select.* entries
from the codegen WASM unsupported-op table.

Problem
-------
hew.select.* ops were unconditionally rejected when targeting wasm32.
The rejection reason ("requires OS threads for blocking") was no longer
accurate: the cooperative ask/reply machinery and the tick-based scheduler
(hew_wasm_sched_tick) already existed and were sufficient to implement
select parity without OS threads.

Implementation
--------------
* hew_reply_wait (WASM): drives the cooperative scheduler one activation
  at a time via hew_wasm_sched_tick(1) until the reply channel becomes
  ready, then returns the value. Returns null if the run queue drains
  before a reply arrives (program-level stall).

* hew_select_first (WASM): scans channels in a loop, yielding one
  scheduler activation between each scan. Returns the index of the
  first ready channel, or -1 when the queue drains with no winner.

Fail-closed invariants preserved
---------------------------------
* Finite timeout (timeout_ms >= 0) is NOT supported. WASI clock_time_get
  is not guaranteed in all sandboxes; faking elapsed time would be
  semantically incorrect. hew_select_first returns -1 immediately for
  any non-negative timeout_ms.
  WASM-TODO: non-zero timeout requires WASI clock_time_get support

* Winner-arm selection: first scan after dispatch finds the lowest-index
  ready channel, matching native behaviour.

* Loser cancellation: hew.select.destroy lowers to hew_reply_channel_free
  (unchanged); the channel is marked cancelled before the loser actor
  processes its message, so hew_reply on a cancelled channel is a clean
  no-op (existing WASM implementation).

* No leaked reply channels: the scheduler drains the loser actor's message
  at hew_sched_shutdown(), which calls hew_reply on the cancelled channel
  and releases the sender-side reference cleanly.

Tests
-----
* 10 unit tests in reply_channel_wasm::tests covering: null inputs,
  single ready channel, multi-channel first-wins, finite-timeout
  fail-closed, and empty-queue stall returns -1.
* New WASM e2e test wasm_select_basic.hew proves end-to-end:
  infinite-wait select on WASM, first arm wins deterministically (FIFO
  cooperative queue), loser cleanup leaves no crash/leak.
* Registered in CMakeLists.txt as add_wasm_file_test(select_basic ...).

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(wasm-select): resolve timeoutMs sentinel, reject timed multi-arm select, clear spurious WASM warning

- MLIRGenExpr.cpp: default timeoutMs from INT32_MAX to -1 so no-timeout
  WASM selects take the infinite-wait cooperative-scheduler path instead
  of triggering the fail-closed timeout_ms>=0 guard in hew_select_first

- MLIRGenExpr.cpp: add early WASM rejection for timed multi-arm select
  (before any hew.select.add emissions) so asks are never enqueued when
  a timeout is present — avoids orphaned asks after timeout fires
  WASM-TODO: multi-arm timed select deferred until WASI clock_time_get

- hew-types/src/check/expressions.rs: stop emitting WasmUnsupportedFeature
  warning for no-timeout selects; they are now fully supported via the
  cooperative scheduler (hew_select_first / hew_reply_wait). Timed multi-arm
  selects still warn (codegen will then hard-error with clear WASM-TODO message).

- hew-types/src/check/types.rs: update Select warning reason text to
  accurately describe the remaining limitation (multi-arm timed select
  requires WASI clock_time_get) rather than the old OS-thread rationale.

- CMakeLists.txt: register add_wasm_reject_test(select_timed ...) for the
  new wasm_reject_select_timed.hew test that verifies the codegen error path

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(clippy): add safety comments, fix doc backtick, suppress cast lints in wasm select tests

- Add SAFETY comments to all unsafe blocks in the new hew_select_first /

- Wrap WASI clock_time_get in backticks in the doc comment (clippy::doc_markdown)

commit.message:
fix: startup Collateral-Churn false positive and PHASE2 blacklist gap

On restart after a collateral migration (or prior squatter attack), the
registry on disk may hold a stale entry for the local node's IP with an
old outpoint.  When the squatter-eviction path calls register() a second
time with the current outpoint, the Collateral-Churn guard in
register() sees the local IP already on-chain with a *different* outpoint
and blocks re-registration (CollateralAlreadyLocked).

Fix: after unregistering the squatter and before the second register()
call, also unregister any stale entry for the local node's own IP.  This
is safe because we are about to immediately re-register with the current
conf outpoint, and reconstruct_reward_counters() rebuilds counters from
blockchain state at startup.

Also adds the ip_blacklist pre-filter to the PHASE2 outbound connect
loop, which already existed in PHASE3 but was missing in PHASE2.
Permanently-banned peers (64.90.3.76 etc.) were completing a full
TCP+TLS round-trip before being dropped on first message.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Add `WASM_MEMORY_ADDR_REL_LEB` Relocation Type (#126676)

This PR contains one of @davidwrighton's commits extracted from #126662
to reduce the PR size and complexity. It adds a relocation type that is
essentially `R_WASM_MEMORY_ADDR_REL_LEB`, so that a relocation
corresponding to a relative offset from r2r `imageBase` can be used
directly as the `offset` in a load, like:
```wasm
global.get $imageBase
i32.load align=... offset=<reloc>
```

The functionality isn't used yet, but will be in #126662.

commit.message:
Simplify TrimmerSingleWarn intermediate assembly update using MSBuild item Update (#125630)

## Description

Follow-up to PR #124801 review feedback: the "intersection via
include/remove, then remove+re-include" pattern in
`_PrepareTrimConfiguration` was complex, mutated item ordering, and used
two throwaway item groups. Replace with a direct MSBuild `Update`.

### Change

**Before** — compute intersection manually to set metadata, then
remove+re-add items:
```xml
<__SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly Include="@(ResolvedFileToPublish)" />
<__SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly Remove="@(IntermediateAssembly)" />
<_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly Include="@(ResolvedFileToPublish)" />
<_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly Remove="@(__SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly)" />
<_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly>
  <TrimmerSingleWarn Condition="...">false</TrimmerSingleWarn>
</_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly>
<ResolvedFileToPublish Remove="@(_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly)" />
<ResolvedFileToPublish Include="@(_SingleWarnIntermediateAssembly)" />
```

**After** — update matching items directly, preserving order:
```xml
<ResolvedFileToPublish Update="@(IntermediateAssembly)">
  <TrimmerSingleWarn Condition=" '%(ResolvedFileToPublish.TrimmerSingleWarn)' == '' ">false</TrimmerSingleWarn>
</ResolvedFileToPublish>
```

## Changes proposed in this pull request

- [`Microsoft.NET.ILLink.targets`] Replace 13-line intersection pattern
with a 3-line `Update` in `_PrepareTrimConfiguration`
- [`Microsoft.NET.ILLink.targets`] Qualify `%(TrimmerSingleWarn)` as
`%(ResolvedFileToPublish.TrimmerSingleWarn)` in the `Update` condition
to prevent MSB4096 (unqualified metadata batching over all
`ResolvedFileToPublish` items, including those without the metadata
defined)

## Additional context

commit.message:
feat: upgrade to 256MB VM images

Switch from 128MB to 256MB microvm images for Nanvix sandbox, CPython,
and QuickJS runtimes on all platforms.

Changes:

- Introduce VM_MEMORY_TIER constant (256mb) used for all platforms
- Update Nanvix, CPython, and QuickJS asset regex patterns to match
  256MB release artifacts
- Derive ramfs size validation limit dynamically from VM_MEMORY_TIER
  (~89MB for 256MB)
- Adapt to new CPython 256MB tarball structure where the guest binary
  (python.elf) is outside sysroot/ rather than at sysroot/bin/python3.12
- Use the Linux CPython tarball on all platforms (stdlib is pure Python,
  guest binary is always ELF regardless of host OS)
- After extraction, locate bin/python.elf and reassemble it into the
  python-sysroot/bin/ directory
- Rename all binary references from python3.12 to python.elf across
  setup, sandbox, platform, tests, and documentation

Motivation:

Upstream releases (nanvix/nanvix, nanvix/cpython, nanvix/quickjs) now
publish 256MB image variants. The larger VM doubles available memory for
guest workloads, enabling heavier Python scripts and reducing the risk
of OOM inside the sandbox.

Fix alternation leftmost-match position bug (#151)

* Add comprehensive tests for alternation leftmost-match position bug

Tests for issue #150: SafeRE returns wrong match positions when
alternation alternatives can match at different starting positions.

Adds AlternationLeftmostPositionTest with 97 tests (19 currently
failing) covering:

- Basic different-position alternation (short/long text)
- Issue #150 exact regression patterns (generics, API version)
- Capturing vs non-capturing groups
- Multiple alternatives, nested alternation
- Quantifiers, character classes, dot wildcards
- Long text (DFA path) with same vs different match endpoints
- Minimal reproductions isolating shared-prefix partial-match behavior
- Exhaustive cross-engine tests with multi-char alternation atoms
- Real-world patterns (HTTP methods, version strings, paths)

The bug manifests in two dimensions:
1. DFA path (long text): fails when alternatives have different endpoints
2. Short text: fails when alt1 partially matches before failing while
   alt2 fully matches at the same position

Refs #150

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* AGENTS.md: clarify test-first workflow for bug fixing

Emphasize that test coverage must come before investigating engine
internals. The workflow is: identify the class of bugs, write systematic
tests, run them, then investigate and fix.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* Fix alternation leftmost-match bug by skipping DFA sandwich

The DFA sandwich (forward DFA → reverse DFA → anchored forward DFA)
incorrectly identifies the match start for alternation patterns when
alternatives match at different positions with different endpoints.
The forward DFA with longest=false returns the earliest match END,
which can come from a later-starting alternative that ends sooner,
causing the reverse DFA to miss the leftmost-starting match.

For example, (bcd|abcde) on text containing 'abcde': the forward DFA
returns end=4 from the 'bcd' match (starting at 1), missing the
leftmost 'abcde' match starting at 0 (which ends at 5).

Fix: mark dfaStartReliable() as false for alternation patterns, so
the DFA sandwich is skipped and the BitState/NFA engine handles the
search. The forward DFA existence check still runs for the fast
negative path.

Fixes #150

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
add read_all_pvts: parse all NAV-PVTs from each FIFO drain

Previously read_pvt() drained up to 512 bytes from the FIFO and returned
only the first NAV-PVT found, silently discarding the rest (up to 4 more
per 512-byte read at 100 bytes/PVT). This caused significant sample loss
whenever the FIFO accumulated multiple packets — e.g. during a 16-second
Notecard transmission at 25 Hz GPS: 40 PVTs queued in chip FIFO, but only
~8 were extracted (1 per 512-byte drain × 8 reads).

Changes:
- Refactor parse_nav_pvt into iter_nav_pvts(buf, &mut f) that calls f()
  for EVERY valid NAV-PVT found in the buffer.  parse_nav_pvt is now a
  thin wrapper (behaviorally unchanged).
- Add MaxM10S::read_all_pvts(i2c, &mut f): drains one chunk and invokes
  f for every packet.  Returns Ok(true) if FIFO had data, Ok(false) when
  empty.  Caller loops until false to fully drain the FIFO.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
